title: Meeting the Terminal & Claude Code
description: Get comfortable opening a terminal, moving around folders, and starting/stopping a Claude Code session.
tip: Run `claude` in your terminal to start a session, and `Ctrl+C` or `exit` to stop it.
content:
Phase 1 — Foundations

Welcome to Week 1! Before we touch Claude Code, we need to feel at home in the terminal. Think of it as learning to walk in a new city before running.

New concepts & commands:

  • What a terminal is and what "the current directory" means
  • pwd — print working directory ("where am I?")
  • ls — list files in the current folder
  • cd — change directory (move into a folder)
  • mkdir — create a new folder
  • Installing Claude Code and logging in
  • Starting Claude Code with claude, exiting with Ctrl+C / exit

This week's exercise:

Create a folder called research-playground on your Desktop, cd into it, and open Claude Code there. Ask it: "What folder am I in and what's in it?" — notice how it answers.

Make it yours: Practice navigating to 2–3 different folders on your computer (Desktop, Documents, a project folder) using only cd and ls — no mouse allowed.
tasks: Open your terminal and run `pwd` — confirm you can see your current folder path
Use `cd` and `ls` to navigate to your Desktop and list its contents
Create a folder called `research-playground` using `mkdir` and `cd` into it
Install Claude Code and run `claude` to start your first session — then exit with `Ctrl+C`

title: Talking to Claude Code
description: Learn the basic conversational loop — asking, reading responses, and referencing files.
tip: Reference any file in your project with @filename so Claude can read it directly.
content:
Phase 1 — Foundations

Claude Code isn't just a chatbot — it can read, create, and modify your files. This week we learn how to have a real conversation with it about your actual documents.

New concepts & commands:

  • Prompting basics: be specific, give context, ask for one thing at a time
  • Referencing a file with @filename to let Claude read it
  • Asking Claude to read, explain, or summarize a file
  • Asking Claude to create a simple text or markdown file

This week's exercise:

Drop a short text file (e.g. a paragraph of notes) into research-playground. Ask Claude to read it (@notes.txt) and summarize it in 3 bullet points. Then ask it to save that summary as a new file.

Make it yours: Take any short document you have — an email, a meeting note — and have Claude summarize it the same way. Compare it to your own mental summary.
tasks: Create a short `notes.txt` file inside `research-playground` with a paragraph of your own writing
Ask Claude to read it using `@notes.txt` and summarize it in 3 bullet points
Ask Claude to save the summary as a new file called `notes-summary.md`
Rewrite your prompt to be more specific and compare the two outputs — notice the difference

title: Projects, Context & Safety
description: Understand how Claude Code "remembers" a project and how to stay in control of what it does.
tip: Use Plan Mode to have Claude propose what it's about to do before it does anything.
content:
Phase 1 — Foundations

Claude Code can take actions on your files — which is powerful but requires trust. This week we learn how to keep control while still letting Claude do useful work.

New concepts & commands:

  • CLAUDE.md — the project's "instructions file" (intro only; we'll use it for real in Week 9)
  • The approval/permission flow: Claude asks before editing or creating files
  • Plan Mode: ask Claude to propose a plan before doing anything
  • Undoing or reverting a change if something goes wrong

This week's exercise:

Ask Claude (in Plan Mode) to propose a folder structure for organizing "research notes." Review the plan together, then approve it and watch it create the folders.

Make it yours: Practice saying no — ask Claude to do something, then reject its proposed plan and ask for a different approach. Notice how it responds.
tasks: Ask Claude to propose a folder structure for "research notes" using Plan Mode — review the plan before approving
Approve the plan and watch Claude create the folders
Ask Claude to make a change, then explicitly reject it and ask for an alternative approach
Create an empty `CLAUDE.md` file in your project folder — you'll fill it in Week 9

title: First Real Research Task
description: Apply everything so far to one real, low-stakes research artifact.
tip: Ask Claude to do one thing at a time: summarize first, then extract quotes, then save — don't bundle it all in one prompt.
content:
Phase 1 — Foundations

Time to put theory into practice. This week we run a complete mini-workflow on a real interview transcript using the skills we've built over the last 3 weeks.

New concepts & commands:

  • Organizing a small project folder (e.g. interview-001/)
  • Asking Claude to extract specific things from a document: quotes, pain points, feature requests
  • Generating a markdown summary report

This week's exercise:

Use one real (or anonymized/sample) interview transcript. Ask Claude to:

  1. Summarize the interview in plain language
  2. Pull out 5 direct quotes about pain points
  3. Save the result as interview-001-summary.md

Make it yours: Repeat the full process with a second transcript entirely on your own — no guidance. Note where you felt confident and where you hesitated.
tasks: Create an `interview-001/` folder and drop in a real or sample transcript
Ask Claude to summarize the interview in plain language
Ask Claude to extract 5 direct quotes about pain points
Ask Claude to save the full summary as `interview-001-summary.md`
Repeat the entire process with a second transcript on your own

title: Working Across Multiple Transcripts
description: Move from processing one document to synthesizing a whole set — the core of research work.
tip: Ask Claude about cross-cutting themes across all files, not just per-document summaries.
content:
Phase 2 — Research Workflow Foundations

Real research rarely lives in a single file. This week we scale up: instead of one transcript, we work with a folder of them and start looking for patterns across participants.

New concepts & commands:

  • Putting multiple transcripts in one folder
  • Asking Claude to read all files in a folder at once
  • Asking for cross-cutting themes vs. per-document summaries

This week's exercise:

With 3 transcripts in a folder, ask Claude: "What themes appear across all three interviews? For each theme, list which participant(s) mentioned it and a representative quote."

Make it yours: Try rephrasing the same request 2–3 different ways and compare the outputs. This builds intuition for prompting — small wording changes can produce very different results.
tasks: Place 3 transcripts in a single folder and ask Claude to read all of them at once
Ask Claude: "What themes appear across all three interviews?" — note themes with participant attribution
Rephrase the same cross-cutting question 2 different ways and compare the outputs
Save the cross-cutting themes as `themes-draft.md`

title: Coding & Tagging Qualitative Data
description: Build a lightweight codebook — the foundation of structured qualitative analysis.
tip: Start with Claude's suggested codes, then refine — it's faster to edit than to build from scratch.
content:
Phase 2 — Research Workflow Foundations

Qualitative coding is the backbone of rigorous research. This week we bring that discipline into Claude Code, creating a structured table of quotes and codes we can actually use.

New concepts & commands:

  • Asking Claude to tag quotes with codes (e.g. "navigation confusion," "pricing concern")
  • Generating a simple CSV or markdown table of quote → code → source
  • Iterating on a codebook: adding, merging, and renaming codes

This week's exercise:

Ask Claude to go through your transcripts and produce a table: quote, suggested code, participant. Review it together and refine 2–3 codes that feel off or too broad.

Make it yours: Add a 4th transcript and ask Claude to apply the same codebook to it — testing for consistency across your dataset.
tasks: Ask Claude to tag quotes from your transcripts with suggested codes — save as a markdown table
Review the table and identify 2–3 codes that feel too broad or misapplied
Ask Claude to refine those codes and regenerate the table
Add a 4th transcript and ask Claude to apply the same codebook — check for consistency

title: Affinity Mapping & Synthesis
description: Go from tagged quotes to grouped insights — a digital affinity map.
tip: Save your synthesis as a structured markdown document so it's easy to share and revisit.
content:
Phase 2 — Research Workflow Foundations

Affinity mapping is where individual observations become shared insights. This week we take last week's tagged data and let Claude help us see the bigger picture.

New concepts & commands:

  • Asking Claude to cluster codes and quotes into higher-level themes
  • Structuring output as a hierarchy: theme → sub-theme → supporting quotes
  • Saving the synthesis as a structured markdown document

This week's exercise:

Using last week's tagged data, ask Claude to group the codes into 3–5 higher-level themes. Have it produce a one-page synthesis document with supporting evidence (quotes) for each theme.

Make it yours: Share this synthesis doc with a teammate and note where it needs human judgment vs. where Claude got it right. That gap is valuable information.
tasks: Ask Claude to cluster your codes from Week 6 into 3–5 higher-level themes
Ask Claude to structure the output as: theme → sub-theme → supporting quotes
Save the result as a one-page `synthesis-v1.md`
Share the document with a teammate and collect one piece of feedback on where it falls short

title: Custom Slash Commands
description: Turn repeated prompts into reusable one-word commands.
tip: Name your commands after the action, not the tool — `/summarize-interview` is clearer than `/run-claude`.
content:
Phase 2 — Research Workflow Foundations

If you find yourself typing the same prompt every week, it's time to automate it. Custom slash commands let you turn any repeatable workflow into a single reusable trigger.

New concepts & commands:

  • What a custom slash command is and where it lives in your project
  • Creating a personal command, e.g. /summarize-interview
  • Adding simple arguments to a command (e.g. passing in a filename)

This week's exercise:

Write a /summarize-interview command that takes a transcript file and produces the Week 4-style summary automatically. Test it on a new transcript you haven't used before.

Make it yours: Create a second command, /tag-quotes, based on the Week 6 coding workflow — your codebook applied automatically.
tasks: Create a `/summarize-interview` command that produces a Week 4-style summary from a transcript
Test the command on a transcript you haven't processed before
Create a `/tag-quotes` command based on your Week 6 coding workflow
Run both commands back-to-back on the same transcript — confirm the output is consistent

title: CLAUDE.md as a Research Assistant Config
description: Give Claude standing instructions so every research task follows your preferences by default.
tip: Include your codebook conventions and file naming rules in CLAUDE.md — Claude reads it automatically at the start of every session.
content:
Phase 2 — Research Workflow Foundations

Tired of reminding Claude how you like things done? CLAUDE.md is your way of teaching it once and having it remember every time. This week we build a real config for research work.

New concepts & commands:

  • Writing a CLAUDE.md for a research project: tone, output format, codebook conventions, file naming
  • How Claude reads this file automatically at the start of every session in that project

This week's exercise:

Write a CLAUDE.md together that includes rules like: "Always output summaries in markdown with this structure... Always use this codebook... Always cite participant IDs, never names." Then re-run a Week 5 task and compare the consistency of the output.

Make it yours: Add 2–3 of your own personal preferences to the file — a specific report template you like, a citation style, a tone of voice.
tasks: Write a `CLAUDE.md` with at least 3 rules: output format, codebook conventions, and file naming
Add a rule to always use participant IDs (P01, P02…) instead of names
Re-run the Week 5 cross-cutting themes task and compare the new output to the old one
Add 2 personal preferences of your own to the config and test them

title: Mini-Project 1: End-to-End Synthesis
description: Independently run a small but complete research synthesis from scratch.
tip: Use everything you've built: your folder structure, your custom commands, your CLAUDE.md. See how far the system takes you before you need to intervene.
content:
Phase 2 — Research Workflow Foundations

This is your first solo flight. No step-by-step guidance — just you, your tools, and a real dataset.

The mission (mostly solo):

Using 3–5 real interviews, work through the full pipeline:

  1. Organize the folder structure
  2. Run your custom commands
  3. Produce a tagged dataset
  4. Create an affinity-style synthesis
  5. Write a final findings document

Use only what you've learned in Weeks 1–9.

Debrief questions:

  • What felt smooth and automatic?
  • Where did you get stuck or fall back to manual work?
  • What would you want to automate next?

Your answers will shape the focus of the next phase.
tasks: Organize 3–5 interviews into the folder structure from Week 3
Run `/summarize-interview` and `/tag-quotes` on every transcript
Produce an affinity-style synthesis using the Week 7 approach
Write a final `findings.md` document — no guidance, no hints
Write a short debrief noting what was smooth and what still felt manual

title: Working with Survey & Spreadsheet Data
description: Bring quantitative data (CSV/Excel survey exports) into the same research workflow.
tip: Ask Claude to connect survey results back to your interview themes — it's surprisingly good at spotting alignment and contradictions.
content:
Phase 3 — Data, Visuals & Deliverables

Real research is mixed-methods. This week we expand beyond interviews and bring in survey data — learning to treat quantitative findings as a companion to qualitative insights.

New concepts & commands:

  • Asking Claude to read a CSV/XLSX and describe its structure
  • Basic descriptive stats: counts, averages, top responses
  • Connecting quantitative findings to qualitative themes from earlier weeks

This week's exercise:

Give Claude a survey export. Ask it to summarize the top 5 responses per question, then ask: "Do any of these results support or contradict the themes from our interview synthesis?"

Make it yours: Ask Claude to flag any survey results that surprised it given the qualitative data. These tensions are often the most interesting findings.
tasks: Share a survey CSV/XLSX with Claude and ask it to describe the structure
Ask Claude to summarize the top 5 responses per question
Ask: "Do any of these results support or contradict the themes from our interview synthesis?"
Ask Claude to flag any results that surprised it — save the tensions as a separate note

title: Visualizing Research Data
description: Turn findings into simple charts and diagrams ready for sharing with stakeholders.
tip: Always ask Claude to propose 2 different ways to visualize a finding — you'll often pick the one you didn't think of first.
content:
Phase 3 — Data, Visuals & Deliverables

A finding buried in a markdown file doesn't drive decisions. Charts and diagrams do. This week we learn to turn research outputs into visuals that communicate at a glance.

New concepts & commands:

  • Asking Claude to generate a chart from survey data
  • Asking Claude to create a simple diagram (e.g. a journey map or theme map)
  • Saving visuals alongside written findings in the same project folder

This week's exercise:

From the survey data, generate one bar chart of a key question's results. Separately, ask Claude to sketch a simple "theme map" diagram summarizing the Week 7 synthesis.

Make it yours: Pick one finding you'd present to stakeholders and have Claude propose 2 different ways to visualize it — then choose the one that communicates more clearly.
tasks: Ask Claude to generate a bar chart from one key survey question
Ask Claude to sketch a theme map diagram summarizing your Week 7 synthesis
Pick one finding and ask Claude to propose 2 different ways to visualize it
Save all visuals into a `04-deliverables/visuals/` folder alongside your written findings

title: Polished Deliverables (Word & Slides)
description: Produce stakeholder-ready outputs — not just markdown notes.
tip: If your team uses a specific report template, describe its structure to Claude in detail and it will match it closely.
content:
Phase 3 — Data, Visuals & Deliverables

Markdown is great for thinking; Word and slides are great for communicating. This week we take our research and package it into formats that work in the real world.

New concepts & commands:

  • Asking Claude to turn a findings document into a Word document
  • Asking Claude to draft a slide outline or deck for a readout
  • Light formatting requests: headings, executive summary, appendix structure

This week's exercise:

Take the Week 10 findings document and ask Claude to produce:

  1. A one-page Word summary
  2. A short slide outline for a team readout

Make it yours: Adjust the tone and structure to match a real template your team uses — if you have one, share it with Claude and ask it to mirror the format.
tasks: Ask Claude to convert your Week 10 `findings.md` into a one-page Word document
Ask Claude to draft a slide outline for a 10-minute team readout
Describe your team's report template to Claude and ask it to reformat the output to match
Review both outputs and note what still needs a human touch before sharing

title: Specialized Subagents for Research Roles
description: Use focused AI "personas" for different parts of the research process.
tip: Give each subagent a very narrow job description — the more focused the role, the more consistent the output.
content:
Phase 3 — Data, Visuals & Deliverables

Not every research task requires the same mindset. A good coder is meticulous; a good synthesizer is a big-picture thinker. This week we set up specialized subagents for each role.

New concepts & commands:

  • What a subagent is: a specialized assistant configured for a specific task
  • Setting up a "coder" subagent (tags quotes with strict codebook rules)
  • Setting up a "synthesizer" subagent (writes executive summaries)
  • When to use subagents vs. just asking directly

This week's exercise:

Set up one subagent focused only on quote-tagging, and one focused only on writing executive summaries. Run the same dataset through both separately, then compare the results to a single general request.

Make it yours: Identify one recurring research task in your workflow that would benefit from a dedicated subagent — something you do often and want done consistently.
tasks: Set up a "coder" subagent with strict codebook rules — keep its role description narrow
Set up a "synthesizer" subagent focused only on writing executive summaries
Run the same dataset through both subagents and compare their outputs
Identify one more recurring task in your workflow that deserves its own dedicated subagent

title: Light Automation
description: Reduce manual repetitive steps in your research workflow.
tip: Time your automated workflow vs. the manual version — the numbers are great for showing value to your team.
content:
Phase 3 — Data, Visuals & Deliverables

The best workflow is one you barely have to think about. This week we remove the friction: instead of running commands one-by-one, we set things up to process entire folders automatically.

New concepts & commands:

  • Simple automation: auto-running a command on new files, batch-processing a folder of transcripts
  • Intro to hooks — just enough to see the concept, not a deep dive

This week's exercise:

Set up a workflow where dropping multiple transcripts into a folder and running one command processes all of them (summary + tags) in one go.

Make it yours: Time how long this takes vs. doing it manually one-by-one. This is a concrete number you can use to show value to your team or stakeholders.
tasks: Set up a workflow that processes all transcripts in a folder with a single command
Test it: drop 3 new transcripts into the folder and run the single command
Time the automated run and compare it to doing the same 3 files manually
Write one sentence in your `CLAUDE.md` describing this automation for future reference

title: Mini-Project 2: Mixed-Methods Pipeline
description: Combine everything: qualitative, quantitative, visuals, and a polished deliverable — all in one run.
tip: Don't start from scratch — use your template folder, your CLAUDE.md, and your custom commands from the very beginning.
content:
Phase 3 — Data, Visuals & Deliverables

This is your second solo project — and the most ambitious yet. You have every tool you need. This week is about proving it to yourself.

The mission (mostly solo):

Using a real or sample dataset (a handful of transcripts + a survey export), independently produce:

  1. A tagged qualitative dataset
  2. A synthesis document with themes and supporting evidence
  3. At least one chart or visual
  4. A polished Word or slide summary

Use your custom commands, your CLAUDE.md, and subagents where they help.

Debrief: Where were the gaps in your system? What still required too much manual effort? These gaps shape Phase 4 — building something you can run independently forever.
tasks: Start from your template folder — don't create a new structure from scratch
Produce a tagged qualitative dataset using your custom commands
Generate at least one chart or visual from the quantitative data
Deliver a polished Word doc or slide deck summary
Write a debrief note listing 2 gaps you'd like to fix in Phase 4

title: Designing a Reusable Research Repo
description: Create a folder and template structure you can reuse for every new research project.
tip: Name folders with numbers (01-, 02-) so they always sort in the order you actually work through them.
content:
Phase 4 — Building Your Own Research System

Starting a new project from a blank folder every time is friction you don't need. This week we design a reusable template so you can hit the ground running on any new study.

New concepts & commands:

  • Designing a standard project structure, e.g.: 
    01-raw-data/
02-tagged/
03-synthesis/
04-deliverables/
  • A reusable CLAUDE.md template for new projects
  • Copying a template folder to start a new project

This week's exercise:

Design and create a template folder together. Test it by simulating a "new fake project" — copy the template, rename it, and go through the motions of starting fresh.

Make it yours: Refine the folder names and structure to match how your team already organizes research, if applicable. Consistency with your team's expectations makes adoption easier.
tasks: Design and create a standard template folder with numbered subfolders (01-, 02-…)
Add a reusable `CLAUDE.md` template inside the folder
Simulate a new project: copy the template, rename it, and start a fake research session from scratch
Adjust the folder names to match your team's existing conventions

title: Your Personal Command Library
description: Consolidate and expand your custom slash commands into a personal research toolkit.
tip: Write a short cheat sheet of your commands and what each does — future-you will thank present-you.
content:
Phase 4 — Building Your Own Research System

By now you have a growing collection of commands. This week we audit, clean up, and expand them — turning a loose collection into a coherent personal toolkit.

New concepts & commands:

  • Reviewing commands built so far: /summarize-interview, /tag-quotes, and others
  • Adding 2–3 new commands based on gaps identified in the mini-projects
  • Organizing commands so they're easy to find and remember

This week's exercise:

Audit your current command library. Fix any commands that feel clunky or produce inconsistent output. Add at least one new command that addresses a gap you noticed in Week 16.

Make it yours: Write a short "cheat sheet" of all your commands and what each does — keep it somewhere you'll actually look at it.
tasks: Audit every command you've built so far — list what works well and what feels clunky
Fix or rewrite at least one command that produces inconsistent output
Add at least one new command addressing a gap from your Week 16 debrief
Write a `commands-cheatsheet.md` listing every command and its purpose in one line

title: Connecting External Tools (MCP)
description: Pull research artifacts from and push outputs to the tools you already use.
tip: Start with whichever tool would save you the most copy-paste time — even one connection changes your daily workflow.
content:
Phase 4 — Building Your Own Research System

Your research doesn't live only in terminal folders — it's spread across Notion, Google Drive, and shared documents. This week we bridge the gap using MCP connectors.

New concepts & commands:

  • What MCP connectors are, at a high level (bridges between Claude Code and external tools)
  • Connecting one relevant tool — Notion, Google Drive, or whatever you actually use
  • Asking Claude to pull a document from that tool, or save output back to it

This week's exercise:

Connect one tool. Try a complete round trip: pull a real document in, do something useful with it (summarize, tag, synthesize), then push a result back out.

Make it yours: Even if you don't set up every connection today, identify which tool would save you the most time if it were connected. That's your priority for the future.
tasks: Identify which external tool (Notion, Google Drive, etc.) wastes the most copy-paste time
Set up an MCP connector for that tool
Complete a round trip: pull a real document in, process it, and push the result back out
Note which other tools you'd connect next — add them to your research backlog

title: Working with Images & Screenshots
description: Bring visual research artifacts — prototype screenshots, usability stills, sketches — into the workflow.
tip: Share 2–3 screenshots at once and ask Claude to compare them — it's surprisingly good at spotting consistency issues.
content:
Phase 4 — Building Your Own Research System

Not all research lives in text. Usability sessions, prototype reviews, and field studies produce images that carry information text can't capture. This week we bring visuals into the workflow.

New concepts & commands:

  • Sharing an image with Claude and asking it to describe or analyze it
  • Asking Claude to compare two screenshots (e.g. before/after a design change)
  • Combining image analysis with written findings in one document

This week's exercise:

Share 2–3 screenshots from a usability session or prototype. Ask Claude to note usability concerns visible in each, then fold that visual analysis into a findings document alongside interview data.

Make it yours: Try this with a real prototype screen you've been testing — the closer to your actual work, the more useful the output.
tasks: Share 2–3 usability session screenshots and ask Claude to describe what it observes
Ask Claude to compare two screenshots and note consistency issues
Ask Claude to incorporate the visual analysis into an existing findings document
Identify one type of image your research regularly produces that Claude could help analyze

title: Tackling Bigger, Messier Datasets
description: Build confidence handling larger or more complex research datasets without losing structure.
tip: Use Plan Mode for big datasets — have Claude propose its analysis approach before executing anything.
content:
Phase 4 — Building Your Own Research System

Not every project is 3 tidy transcripts. Real research can be 15 interviews, multiple survey waves, and stakeholder notes all mixed together. This week we build the confidence to handle that scale.

New concepts & commands:

  • Extended thinking for complex, multi-step analysis requests
  • Using Plan Mode for large tasks: "Here's 15 transcripts — propose how you'd approach synthesis before doing it"
  • Breaking a large task into stages and executing one at a time

This week's exercise:

Take the largest dataset you have access to (real project, anonymized if needed). Use Plan Mode to have Claude propose an analysis approach, refine the plan together, then execute it in stages.

Make it yours: Reflect on how this differs from your usual manual process — what's meaningfully faster, and what still needs your human judgment?
tasks: Take the largest dataset you have and use Plan Mode to ask Claude to propose an analysis approach
Refine the proposed plan — push back on at least one step and ask for a revision
Execute the analysis in stages, approving each step before moving to the next
Reflect: what was faster than expected, and what still needed your human judgment?

title: Version Control & Collaboration Basics
description: Learn just enough Git to keep a history of your research work and share it with teammates.
tip: Think of `git commit` as hitting Save in a video game — it creates a checkpoint you can always return to.
content:
Phase 4 — Building Your Own Research System

You don't need to become a developer to use Git. You just need enough to protect your work from accidental changes and share it with teammates. That's exactly what this week covers.

New concepts & commands:

  • What Git is for: a "save history" for folders and files
  • git init — turn a folder into a tracked repository
  • git add — stage files to be saved
  • git commit — create a checkpoint with a message
  • Viewing history to see what changed and when
  • Sharing a project folder with a teammate

This week's exercise:

Turn your template repo from Week 17 into a Git repo. Make a deliberate change to one file, commit it with a descriptive message, and look at the history together.

Make it yours: The goal here is comfort, not mastery. You don't need to learn branching or merging — just enough to feel safe making changes.
tasks: Run `git init` inside your Week 17 template folder to make it a Git repository
Make a deliberate change to one file, then run `git add` and `git commit` with a clear message
Run `git log` to view your history — confirm the checkpoint was saved
Share the folder with a teammate and walk them through what you've set up

title: Assembling "The System"
description: Put every piece together into one coherent, documented personal research system.
tip: Writing the documentation is the test — if you can explain it clearly, you actually understand it.
content:
Phase 4 — Building Your Own Research System

All the pieces exist. This week we assemble them into one coherent system — and document it so you (and future teammates) can use it without thinking.

This week is mostly review & integration:

  • Reviewing: template repo, CLAUDE.md config, command library, any subagents, any MCP connectors
  • Writing a short "how I use Claude Code for research" guide for yourself (and future teammates)

This week's exercise:

Walk through the full system end-to-end on a small new dataset — from raw transcripts to polished deliverable — using only your own templates, commands, and config. Then write a one-page guide documenting the system as it stands today.

Make it yours: Identify 1–2 things you'd still like to improve. These become optional follow-ups after Week 24 — your personal research and development backlog.
tasks: Run a full end-to-end workflow on a small new dataset using only your templates, commands, and config
Write a one-page `system-guide.md` that documents every tool in your stack and how they connect
Review your `CLAUDE.md` and command library — update anything that's drifted or become outdated
List 1–2 improvements for your backlog — things to tackle after Week 24

title: Capstone: Independent Research Project
description: Demonstrate full independence and set yourself up for continued growth beyond this program.
tip: Keep iterating on your CLAUDE.md and command library after today — the best research systems grow with the researcher.
content:
Phase 4 — Building Your Own Research System

This is it. No guidance, no exercises, no facilitator looking over your shoulder. Just you and your system — the one you've been building for 23 weeks.

The capstone:

Run one complete research workflow — from raw data to stakeholder-ready deliverable — entirely on your own, using your personal system, on a real or realistic project.

Wrap-up reflection:

  • What parts of the system worked exactly as intended?
  • What would you change for the next project?
  • What do you want to learn next? (Deeper automation? More MCP connectors? Advanced visualizations?)

Where to go from here:

The learning doesn't stop at Week 24. Keep exploring the Claude Code documentation, keep refining your CLAUDE.md, and keep adding to your command library as your workflow evolves. You've built a system — now make it yours.

🎉 Congratulations on completing the program.
tasks: Run a complete research workflow — raw data to stakeholder deliverable — entirely on your own
Write a wrap-up reflection: what worked, what you'd change, what you want to learn next
Share your deliverable with at least one stakeholder or teammate and collect feedback
Commit to one next step: a new command, a new connector, or a technique to explore
